data.title: Philip Morris: Time To Take Profits
data.description:
Philip Morris International (NYSE:PM) has shown extraordinary resilience during the April sell-off and in the months that follow, causing shares to surge to new highs. In my opinion, Philip Morris’ earnings potential is now more than fully reflected in its earnings-based valuation factor and I have decided to take profits and recycle my investment proceeds into British American Tobacco (BTI) for a higher yield and potentially better capital return potential. In my opinion, Philip Morris is going to continue to generate strong EPS growth going forward, but shares are extremely richly priced, especially in direct comparison to other tobacco stocks. Due to valuation considerations – PM is now trading in excess of 21X FY 2026 earnings – I believe locking in profits is the smart thing to do.

Previous Rating

Philip Morris was a hold position for me in my last work in April 2025 – No Margin Of Safety – because of the company’s tilt towards fast-growing emerging markets. The strategic focus on rapidly expanding emerging markets generated the precondition for above-average EPS growth for the tobacco company, and Philip Morris was returning a decent amount of cash back to shareholders. However, PM is now priced well above my fair value estimate, making this a perfect time to sell into the strength and lock in a handsome profit.

Strong Alternative Products Growth Now Fully Baked Into PM's Valuation

Philip Morris’ business is growing, despite secular challenges to the company’s core combustible business. However, since Philip Morris has strong exposure to emerging markets where combustible adoption is still strong, and the tobacco company is seeing rapid expansion in its alternative products category – especially IQOS, ZYN and VEEL (the firm's e-vapor brand) – I believe that Philip Morris' valuation factor leaves little room for more upside.

Alternative products, like ZYN-branded nicotine pouches, IQOS heated tobacco items and VEEP vape products, are key growth drivers at Philip Morris and the company has seen a massive boost to its volume growth in the last year. In the first-quarter, the tobacco company benefited from a boost to its shipments volume of 3.9% Y/Y as well as more than 10% organic revenue growth, mainly due to new smoke-free products. In Q1'25, about 42% of all revenue in Philip Morris' portfolio came from its smoke-free, alternative products, showing 2 PP growth compared to the previous quarter.

The company's alternative product categories are therefore also leading the company's portfolio in terms of growth. In fact, new products in the smoke-free category are driving Philip Morris' entire top line, and the tobacco company has seen some considerable upside momentum in terms of gross profits as well. In the most recent quarter, Philip Morris generated solid 20.4% Y/Y revenue growth in this specific category in Q1'25 with its gross profits growing in excess of 33%.

Despite fundamental challenges to Philip Morris' core business model – selling traditional tobacco products – the company has been able, with the help of new products like IQOS, ZYN or VEEV, to accelerate its shipment volume growth. As a result, the tobacco firm is seeing overall favorable revenue and income trends in its business. In the last three years, Philip Morris' net income surged 16.6% while its revenue grew by about 25%. While Philip Morris is growing at a healthy clip, I believe the company is now overvalued based off of its expected earnings.

Dividend Growth Rates: BTI Vs. Tobacco Rivals

Altria (MO) still has the best dividend growth in the industry group and out-performed both Philip Morris as well as British American Tobacco: Altria generated close to 19% dividend growth in the last five years compared to growth rate of 12.5% for Philip Morris and 8.6% for British American Tobacco. From a dividend growth perspective, the tobacco stock to own would be Altria... which I discussed at length here: A Solid Dividend Play.

Recycling Proceeds From PM Into BTI

Philip Morris is now trading at a 21.3X forward earnings multiplier, which is more than twice as much as what the earnings potential of British American Tobacco is valued at. I therefore decided to sell my shares in Philip Morris this week and recycle my proceeds into BTI, which is not only trading at a much lower earnings multiplier of 9.9X FY 2026 earnings, but also has a much higher dividend yield.

Philip Morris' yield is down to 3.2%, due to massive expansion in the company's P/E ratio, compared to 6.3% for British American Tobacco. I like BTI for both its momentum with its own oral nicotine products, named Velo, and its capital return potential, which I discussed here: A Cheap And Juicy Yield. Since BTI is trading at less than half the P/E ratio than PM, while also having considerable momentum in its alternative products category, I believe investors could potentially earn higher capital returns from British American Tobacco going forward.

In my last work on Philip Morris, I calculated a fair value of $107, based off of a historical P/E ratio of ~15X for the tobacco company. Today, shares of Philip Morris trade in excess of $175, so are, according to my calculation, overvalued by approximately 64%. At the current time, I believe Philip Morris' alternative products momentum is more than fully realized in the firm's valuation and I therefore see an unfavorable risk profile, especially for longer term investors.

Risks With Philip Morris

The biggest risk for Philip Morris, as I see it, relates to slowing momentum in emerging markets as well as in weakening adoption of new smoke-free products like ZYN or IQOS that currently driving new category revenue and gross profit momentum. What would change my opinion on the tobacco company is if the firm were to trade at a much more reasonable valuation factor (~15X forward earnings) or saw an acceleration in its earnings growth.

Final Thoughts

Philip Morris is growing its revenue, its net income and dividend despite secular challenges to the tobacco industry. The emerging market focus as well as the development of smoke-free products has prevented Philip Morris from a decline in its top line and earnings metrics. However, shares are now so expensive that even Philip Morris’ above-average earnings growth is more than fully baked in the tobacco company’s valuation. In my opinion, it could make sense to recycle proceeds into a lower-P/E tobacco firm, even under the assumption of slower annual EPS growth, given BTI's much higher yield and equally strong momentum in new product sales. British American Tobacco's low P/E ratio also reflects a much higher safety margin compared to Philip Morris in case the company were to see a deterioration of fundamentals (vape bans, slowing new product adoption, weaker volume shipments) in the alternative products business.

data.title: Hard To Imagine Retirement Income Without These 2 Cash Machines
data.description:
As for an income investor, who wants to become financially independent by having a portfolio that funds living expenses just through the generated current income, the journey to get there is quite challenging.

For example, the condition to not rely on portfolio holding sales adds a notable headwind in terms of building up a sufficient portfolio size from which to capture the necessary level of income. Furthermore, without any doubt, the living expenses tend to inflate, which means that the portfolio income has to not only fulfill the pre-defined target, but also deliver some growth in order to preserve the purchasing power. On top of that, if there is a "no asset sale" policy in place, then experiencing an income reduction is fully unacceptable, which, in turn, requires being smart and prudent about investment choices.

However, it is of course not just limitation and negatives. There are multiple benefits from this kind of portfolio objective, which, in my view, make it worth doing the heavy work:

  • Reduced longevity risk (i.e., not outliving the asset base).
  • Opportunity to pass a stable source of income to the next generation (and not just leftovers from non-divested portfolio).
  • Less stressful period of financial independence, where market volatility and asset price risks are taken out of the equation.

To achieve this, the general idea is to invest in securities with as high yields as possible while avoiding income reduction and value decay risks.

It is not easy to balance this as the tendency is of course to consider quite high dividend yielders, which come with higher risks accordingly.

So, the sweet spot area is high-quality value businesses/securities, which offer enticing dividends because of "democratic" valuation multiples. These multiples are lower because there is no expectation by the market that they will deliver strong (Mag 7 like) returns. In the context of the above-mentioned objective, this should not be an issue.

I will share now two high-yielding picks, which, in my view, should be placed at the core of portfolios that seek to deliver on such an objective.

#1 - UTF, 6.9% yield

Cohen & Steers Infrastructure Fund (NYSE:UTF) is a closed end fund, which yields almost 7% and distributes the income on a monthly basis.

UTF has an excellent track-record in terms of delivering stable current income since the moment when it was brought to the public market in 2004. This period includes such market shocks as in the GFC and COVID-19. And what is very important here is that all of these consistent dividends have been distributed without utilizing ROC (return of capital) factor in a way that would impair the underlying value.

It is also true that UTF has not increased the payments since 2017, and, in my view, expecting this to happen (or investing with such an assumption) would not be a smart idea. This is because the Fund really strives to keep both dividends and NAV-preservation in check and with a sufficient margin of safety so that when the markets go down like, say, in the COVID-19, the investors would still be able to collect the planned cash flows.

This goes perfectly in line with the strategy or objectives that I outlined above - i.e., high and durable dividends without value decay.

Now, what has, however, happened in the past year is that UTF has experienced a strong price recovery, which has led to a dividend yield normalization - i.e., convergence to the historical average of around 7%.

In my view, this does not mean that we are buying UTF at an unfavorable moment. Let me quickly provide you with several reasons why this is the case.

First, the lion's share of UTF's portfolio is linked to utility, midstream, telecommunication and other non-cyclical infrastructure assets, which currently play an increasingly important role for portfolio protection. The overall stock market trades at peak valuations, while the level of uncertainty in the system is, arguably, the highest that we have had since the COVID-19 or when the Fed suddenly decided to venture into an aggressive interest rate hiking cycle. In this kind of environment, it is only logical that we pay an extra premium for safety.

Second, it is not only the safety aspects that stem from the non-cyclical and durable income exposure to infrastructure that has motivated investors to flock into UTF. The beauty of most infrastructure assets is that they carry inflation-linked cash flows, which clearly allow de-risking the unpredictable effects from the tariff-driven policy. Plus, it adds another layer of safety for UTF in terms of covering the dividend from net investment income generation.

Third, UTF has taken the extra mile for protecting the underlying asset base and enhancing the sustainability of its monthly distributions. What I mean by that is that there is a significant bias towards investment grade companies. For example, almost the entire Top 10 holdings list is saturated with investment grade balance sheets (and the Top 10 explains ~34% of the total exposure).

UTF is one of those cases, where I feel extremely confident about the future cash flows, which is critical for achieving a "low stress" financial independence.

#2 - PFFA, 9.7%

Virtus InfraCap U.S. Preferred Stock ETF (NYSEARCA:PFFA) plays a different ball game for producing monthly cash flows. It yields 9.7%, which is more than UTF and is fully logical given the higher risk profile.

However, let me say that a higher risk profile does not mean that the risks have exceeded a level that would no longer qualify for durable income and value stability.

The risks come from the fact that PFFA's portfolio largely consists of non investment grade securities, from which around 50% are placed in financial and real estate segments. The combination of non investment grade and cyclical factors does indeed sound aggressive.

But here is the thing.

As opposed to UTF and many other securities, which offer this kind of yield, PFFA has an exclusive focus on preferred share. The essence of preferred shares is that they have inherently lowers risks than equities because they rank higher in the capital structure.

Namely, for a company to stop paying cash to its preferred shareholders, it has to first fully get rid of the common dividend payment.

Then it has to think twice whether it really makes sense to decrease preferred share payments over issuing additional equity because if they go with the former scenario, it would imply an automatic increase in the cost of capital and make new lenders (refinancers) less willing to have any exposure.

Finally, since preferred shares come with fixed payments with pre-set payment dates that have to be serviced, companies cannot just decide what level to pay or not (unless they run in a significant distress and there are no other pragmatic options). This improves the cash flow stability for PFFA.

Moreover, we have to appreciate that PFFA is an actively managed ETF with a modest leverage of 20% to 30% depending on the market circumstances. In my view, to navigate around the sector concentration risk, it is extremely important to cherry-pick the right exposures with a limited downside (income reduction) risk. In other words, it is generally a solid strategy to mitigate sector bias risks with an active security selection process. Also, the 20% to 30% leverage component could enhance the cash flows as the high-probability interest rate cuts take place in the foreseeable future (via cheaper leverage service costs).

Finally, if we look at the dividend payment history all the way since the IPO in 2018, I would say that PFFA has delivered on its objective to distribute enticing monthly cash flows, while preserving the underlying NAV. The Fund has paid out a stable dividend 6 years in a row, but suffered a slight dividend drop right in the peak of COVID-19, which, in my view, is acceptable given the ensuing dividend increases, positive total returns and, importantly, almost 10% yield.

The bottom line

Maximizing yield per unit of risk is a challenging exercise, especially if income reduction and invested capital decay are defined as no-go scenarios.

In the article, I have outlined two instruments, which offer current income streams that should move the needle for most income investors. As discussed, the probability of income cut and/or permanent capital decay risk materialization is extremely low given how robust the underlying fundamentals are.

In my humble opinion, both of these securities could be easily included in the core of retirement income focused portfolios.

data.title: Universal Robina: Refocusing On The ASEAN Region Can Deliver Growth, But Price Is No Longer Attractive
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Universal Robina Corporation (OTC:UVRBY)(OTC:UVRBF) is one of the leading consumer food and beverage companies in the Philippines. URC is organized into two operating segments: Branded Consumer Foods [BCF] and Agro-Industrial and Commodity [AIC]. BCF's portfolio comprises a diverse range of products, including snack foods, biscuits, ready-to-drink teas and coffees, noodles, and many others. AIC, meanwhile, sells commodity products such as flour, sugar, and meat.

After URC's disappointing Oceania venture, the company has refocused on the ASEAN market, which, I believe, is a sound strategic shift due to the leadership position they possess in this region. However, despite this bright prospect, I believe that URC's current valuation is unattractive when compared to alternative assets such as the PSEi index. Therefore, I will call a 'Hold' rating for this stock.

FY 2024 Result Discussion

In 2024, URC reported a revenue of ₱161.9B, an increase of 3% compared to last year. EBIT and net income would stand at ₱16.7B and ₱12.2B, down by 4% and 3%, respectively.

The YoY decline in EBIT and net income is due to the URC AIC segment, which saw its EBIT declining by -34% YoY, from a record high ₱8.5B to ₱5.6B. This decline can be attributed to the falling price of sugar, which significantly affected the AIC Sugar and Renewables (SURE), its sugar manufacturing arm.

This decline is a typical correction phase of a commodity cycle. Due to the sugar crisis experienced by the Philippines from 2022 to 2023, sugar manufacturers' production has finally caught up with the demand, thereby correcting the up cycle back to the mean. I expect normalized performance from AIC this year, as supply is being monitored closely by the Philippine Sugar Regulatory Administration (SRA) to protect the interests of farmers and other stakeholders. Excluding the sugar segment, URC's consolidated operating income would've grown by 20%, indicating strong performance from its branded segment. Turning over to the performance of its branded segment, while BCF's revenue grew by only 1.58% YoY, this was offset by the significant growth in the group's consolidated EBIT, which grew by 18.8% YoY.

What impressed me is the result of the BCF international segment, whose EBIT grew by an incredible 43% YoY, indicating that the management's strategy to refocus on ASEAN is gaining traction. I will discuss this in detail later.

Another notable 2024 event for the company was the company's exit from China. I believe this is a good move as the company has never had market leadership in this country. Divesting from China will allow the company to refocus the management's attention and company resources on geographic markets, where they have a market leadership position, specifically the Philippines, Vietnam, Thailand, and Malaysia.

For the year 2025, URC is targeting mid to high-single revenue growth and a net profit growth of a few points behind.

From A Failed Oceania Venture To A More Focused Strategy In ASEAN

In 2014, URC acquired Griffin Food Ltd., a leading snack food company in New Zealand, for approximately ₱26.5 billion or NZ$700M. This transaction was the largest acquisition by URC up to that date. 2 years later, URC would again do another acquisition by acquiring Snackbrand, a market leader in salty snacks in Australia, for a sum of around ₱21.34B or AUD$600M.

From the outset, these acquisitions should be value accretive to URC, as they didn't pay an absurd price. The EV/EBITDA multiple that was used on the Griffin acquisition is around 9x, while around 10x for Snackbrand, which is significantly lower than URC's 24x EV/EBITDA multiple during those times. Additionally, URC acquired a market leader in their respective food categories, meaning both acquirees have a significant brand presence and economies of scale in manufacturing and distribution in the New Zealand and Australian markets.

However, the favorable traits of this deal aren't enough to overcome the dilemma of acquisitions. Despite all the favorable attributes there are to a corporate acquisition, a favorable price, and a strong market leader, in 2016 and onwards, URC's return on capital and margin would decline.

This underperformance suggests that its rationale to leverage its existing ASEAN distribution to bolster these new acquisitions' growth plan didn't pan out as expected. Additionally, analysts have attributed this failure to the changing consumer preference from salty and sweet snacks to healthy ones and the maturity of the Oceania market.

URC would later consolidate its New Zealand and Australia segments into one operating segment and enter a joint venture with Intersnack Group by selling 40% of the consolidated segment. And eventually would fully divest it in 2021 for a loss of around ₱16B out of the ₱47.7B paid on both acquisitions. This does not include the opportunity cost of capital paid on the transaction, which, based on my estimate, will be around ₱7.55B if it were invested in the long-term Philippine government bond yielding around 4% around that time. In summary, the total loss of this venture is around ₱23.55B.

URC would later use the proceeds from the divestiture of its Oceania segment to acquire Munchy for a sum of ₱22.8B, which is a biscuits market leader in Malaysia. The rationale of the acquisition isn't that much different from that of Griffin Foods and Snackbrands. Munchy under URC would gain access to the company's extensive ASEAN distribution, manufacturing, research, and development capabilities outside of its home market. Additionally, the acquisition has reinforced the company's strategy to refocus on the ASEAN region, which they believe is a more attractive market due to its high-growth potential. Since refocusing on the ASEAN region, URC's financial performance has improved.

I believe that URC is refocusing on the ASEAN region and reinforcing that commitment by acquiring Munchy will deliver superb growth and shareholder value creation. Unlike the URC's first high-sticker-price acquisition involving Griffin Foods and Snackbrands, Munchy is a more focused acquisition. The Griffin and Snackbrands acquisition involves multiple consumer food categories (salty snacks, sweets, and crackers), while Munchy only involves one line of food category (biscuits). Munchy's focus on the biscuit food category will allow the URC management to put a significant amount of attention on making sure that their strategy for the Munchy brand, or the reason why they acquired it in the first place, becomes a success. The principle of why I like companies that refocus their business is that management's attention is a scarce resource. It is better spent on ventures where the company has a better chance of winning, which usually means where they are a leader in or where their core capabilities currently are. Additionally, the Munchy acquisition strengthens URC's biscuit portfolio, especially in the Philippines, where they are currently 3rd place in market share.

URC intends to play in the higher price segment of the Philippine biscuit market, which, I believe, makes sense as there is a tendency of premiumization as consumer purchasing power improves. This also presents a margin expansion opportunity for the company as they could charge a significant premium for this segment.

After 2 years of integrating Munchy into URC, the management believes that it's now ready to aggressively push the brand into other countries, outside of Malaysia. I believe this focus and more coherent approach this time by URC has a better chance of being a success.

Suboptimal Capital Structure

One of the assessments that an investor needs to consider when investing in a company, especially in Philippine equities, is: Does its capital structure make sense considering the circumstances of the business? The principle that I use is: 'If demand is predictable and stable, capitalize the balance sheet with more debt with adequate equity, and if demand is cyclical or unpredictable, then maintain a strong balance sheet by maintaining a high amount of equity and minimal debt to protect the shareholders against market downturns. There is some sort of subjectivity in determining the appropriate mix between equity and debt that a company should have. But generally, you don't want a company in a slow-moving industry with predictable and stable demand, such as retail and utilities, to be carrying a significant amount of equity relative to its total assets. Conversely, you don't want a historically cyclical industry or a fast-moving industry, such as semiconductors and technology stocks, to carry a significant amount of debt relative to its total assets. The end goal of an optimal capital structure is to maximize free cash flow generation using the cheapest source of capital that the company could utilize(debt is cheaper than equity) while protecting the shareholders from unexpected market downturns.

Analyzing URC's capital structure, there is a nuance that an investor needs to consider when assessing the company's capital structure. And that is the presence of a commodity business, its AIC segment, alongside its BCF, which is its franchise brand and is a differentiated segment. Now, there wouldn't be a problem if AIC only caters to the demand of the BCF segment, as URC could treat this segment as a hedge against supply chain disruption, and its only purpose is to ensure that the BCF segment has a reliable source of raw materials for its product. However, AIC also sells its products in the open market, making it exposed to the cyclical nature of a commodity market.

Nevertheless, let's see the capital structure of both segments.

Following the principle above, due to the predictability on the demand side for BCF, as most categories under BCF's portfolio can be deemed as recession-proof and are a differentiated business, this segment should be capitalized with more debt. In contrast, AIC should be capitalized with equity due to its exposure to the commodity cycle. Therefore, while I believe the capital structure of the AIC segment is appropriate, I believe the capital structure of the BCF is very conservative and suboptimal. Additionally, when comparing URC BCF's capital structure to similar companies in its industry, you will see a clear disparity.

When compared to companies like Kellanova (K), PepsiCo (PEP), and Nestlé (OTCPK:NSRGY), all have very strong product portfolios and whose demand is inherently stable and predictable, we can further conclude that URC BCF's capital structure is suboptimal. Now, why is an optimal capital structure important for URC? For companies like URC, whose consolidated return on capital employed is marginally better than the average cost of capital in the Philippines of around 10% (Cost of Debt is 6% + Equity Risk Premium of 4% to 6%), but have stable and predictable sales and profits, they could amplify their return on equity by carrying more debt. While carrying debt always has a risk associated with it, it is a cheaper source of capital than equity, and the stability of their cash flow or profits can more than compensate for this risk.

This is not a common issue in the US equities, as the capital market in the US is far more mature and efficient. Typically, companies that have suboptimal capital structure or ones that carry lots of cash in the balance sheet will attract activist investors to force the company to correct these anomalies. However, this is a notorious problem in the Philippines. The majority of the good companies in the Philippines are family controlled, and external market forces that are available in the US to correct these anomalies aren't available. Lots of companies in the Philippines often retain a significant amount of their earnings while paying minuscule dividends, even though the company is generating average or poor returns on those retained earnings.

One of my mitigating approaches to this problem is to avoid these types of companies altogether. Or stick to ones that return a superb return on capital while paying generous dividends, even though they have a suboptimal capital structure. This is just the dilemma of a small investor in the Philippines who has no significant influence on the company in which they have a stake or can't rely on influential investors that share their investment principles to correct these anomalies for them; it's a game of balancing the negatives and the positives and making sure that the upside of the positives outweighs the downside of the negatives.

Valuation

Since plunging to around ₱60 per share, URC's stock has recovered and now sells at around ₱90 per share. This translates to around 16x P/E ratio, a premium compared to the P/E ratio multiple of the PSEi index, which stands at around 11x to 12x. While I'm optimistic about URC's prospects moving forward, this is not the price that I'm willing to pay as it doesn't have a reasonable margin of safety to compensate for the uncertainty of this growth. After all, no matter how sure an investor is about a company's prospects, the future is always uncertain; thus, a margin of safety is always necessary to cushion you against unexpected outcomes. However, suppose the price sells at around the same or close to the valuation multiple of the PSEi index, or at around a 12x to 13x P/E ratio . In that case, it is a fair price to pay, considering the potential of Munchy delivering growth for the company moving forward, and the company's leadership position in the ASEAN region, especially in the Philippines. This is not the type of company that will sell at a significant discount to the overall market. Therefore, I will call a 'Hold' rating for URC.

Where To Buy Universal Robina Corporation Stock

Whenever my article is about a Philippine stock, I will put a special section here for US investors looking to invest in the Philippine stock market. To invest in URC, you have two options: Invest in URC ADR through a broker like IBKR, look for the stock ticker symbol of 'UVRBY' or 'UVRBF', or directly in the Philippine stock exchange, or PSE, by depositing your money with a trusted Philippine stockbroker such as COL Financial with a stock ticker of 'URC.' Now, if you can stomach the volatility due to low volume and liquidity and will invest for the long term without looking at the stock price, then go for the ADR. Suppose you like looking at your portfolio every day and can't stand an overnight double-digit decline. In that case, you can invest directly by depositing your money with a Philippine brokerage account. Again, COL is a trustworthy and reliable broker, and they have operated since 1999. They are doing this annual premium event for investors who deposited $20,000 or ₱1M. The liquidity and volume in the Philippine stock market, especially the highly popular stocks such as URC, are high; the bid-and-ask spread is very close. They are one of the most trusted brokers in the Philippines. Also, these stockbrokers are heavily regulated by the Philippine SEC and PSE.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: The First Inklings Of Stagflation Appear
data.description:
Investors keep pumping money into the stock market near all-time highs with seemingly little concern for anything other than the fear of missing out on the next all-time high. The largest technology stocks that took the brunt of Tuesday's selling were back in the driver's seat yesterday, but the rotation into smaller and more speculative names continued, with the Russell 2000 leading all major market indexes higher. President Trump stoked the flames with the announcement he had reached a trade deal with Vietnam, but that does little to relieve my concerns about the period of modest stagflation that I see ahead.

The consensus is clearly not worried about the economic outlook, given that the S&P 500 is back to the upper end of its historical valuation. That expresses a lot of confidence that I don't have. Instead, I see signs of stagflation, which, I think, could persist well into the fall of this year. The first inkling of this was yesterday's ADP employment report, showing a loss of 33,000 jobs in June. While not due to layoffs, according to ADP's chief economist, the fact that employers are not replacing workers who have quit is a worrying sign. This number likely portends a decline in payrolls reported by the BLS, as well as a rise in the unemployment rate, in the not-too-distant future.

The reason the labor market is weakening is that consumer spending is starting to taper off, as we saw in the personal spending report for May. Consumer spending is what fuels job creation and drives 70% of our economic growth. Spending is starting to stall just as tariffs start to bite.

One of my daughters approached me yesterday in dismay, questioning why she was charged an extra $42 for the purchase of a used pair of sunglasses she bought on eBay this week. She paid $80 for the glasses and $20 for international shipping, so her introduction to trade policy increased the cost by 42%. After explaining that the de minimis exemption expired in May, she informed me that she had made a similar purchase a couple of weeks ago with no surcharge. My suspicion was that US Customs is still ramping up its ability to process the millions of smaller items that are imported each day. The bottom line is that this frequent shopper with a six-figure income has no intention of buying similar items if tariffs are charged. While this may be an anecdotal story, I am pretty sure that shoppers with far fewer means than her are feeling the same way. If so, it helps explain the deceleration in consumer spending, which is likely to worsen in the months ahead.

On July 1, the Atlanta Fed lowered its estimate for GDP growth to 2.5%. The gradual decline in its estimate since May 30 has been due primarily to slower consumer spending growth (PCE), which was just 0.5% in the first quarter and has fallen from 3.3% to 1.5% in the second.

A slower rate of spending growth will exacerbate the softening of the labor market we are already seeing today. Spending is what fuels job growth. Normally, this would promote disinflation, but today we must contend with the impact that import taxes will have on producer and consumer prices, which is a huge unknown. The Fed's preferred inflation gauge rose to 2.7% in May from an upwardly revised 2.6% in April. This could be an inflection point to a core PCE rate that settles later this year north of 3%. That is a legitimate risk this market is not accounting for today. It is why Fed officials remain cautious about further monetary policy easing.

There is no chance that we see 1970s style stagflation, but we could see a tempered version where the unemployment rate ticks up closer to 5%, the rate of economic growth slows to 1%, and the rate of inflation creeps above 3%. That may not lead to a recession or a bear market, but it is not the nirvana that the major market indexes are reflecting today.

data.title: Innovative Industrial Properties: High Risk With High Return - Contrarian Buy For The Brave
data.description:
IIPR's Oversold Position Triggers A Contrarian Buy Opportunity For Those Seeking Rich Dividends & Double Digits Capital Appreciation

We previously covered Innovative Industrial Properties (NYSE:IIPR) in May 2025, discussing how the stock had already lost a great deal of its value from the 52-week heights, attributed to the numerous tenant default issues and the headwinds from illicit cannabis players.

Combined with the reduced likelihood of a cannabis federal legalization during the new US administration period, the potentially impacted top/ bottom-line performance arising from tenant default issues, and the deteriorating cannabis market, we had downgraded the stock to a hold then.

IIPR 1Y Stock Price

Since then, IIPR has mostly traded sideways at +1.8% compared to the wider market at +9.7%, with a similar stock price trend also observed in its cannabis REIT peer in varying degrees.

These naturally underscore the market's uncertainties surrounding the cannabis industry's prospects, attributed to the risks from the intense competition from illicit players, which currently comprises "75 percent of the known marijuana market," with it already contributing to the price compression and the profit margin headwinds.

For example, while regulators may report a robust 2024 legal cannabis sales of $4.66B in California, readers must also note that this number only comprises 40% of the cannabis sold in the state, with the balance coming from illicit players.

Despite the notable improvement from the 25% ratio reported in 2019, we can understand why licensed players may find it extremely difficult to report sustainable top/ bottom-line performance thus far, worsened by the hefty effective 280E federal tax rate at ~70% or higher.

This headwind builds upon the maturing cannabis industry in the US, with 2025 expected to bring forth a decelerating market size growth to $45.35B (+5.5% YoY) compared to the 5Y historical CAGR of +17.3%.

The decelerating trend is partly attributed to most US states already legalizing medicinal cannabis use albeit with mixed recreational use as of April 28, 2025, with it already contributing to the industry's mixed projected growth profile over the next few years.

On the other hand, it appears that we may see recovery tailwinds in the near-term, aided by the recently introduced States 2.0 Act in April 2025, with it expected to eliminate the 280E tax and consequently, drastically improve the licensed players' bottom-lines.

While federal rescheduling is likely to remain prolonged, we believe that the industry's prospects may lift indeed, IIPR's included.

For reference, the cannabis REIT has had numerous tenant default issues over the past few years, with 27.8% of its rental revenues at risk while contributing to the notably impacted FQ1'25 rental revenues of $71.69M (-6.5% QoQ/ -4.2% YoY) and AFFO per share of $1.94 (-12.6% QoQ/ -12.2% YoY).

With all of the defaulting tenants' security deposits already exhausted, aside from the Gold Flora Leases tenant, readers may want to brace for more impact indeed.

We believe that IIPR's FQ2'25 results are likely to be sequentially impacted on the top/ bottom-lines, based on the FQ1'25 revenue benefit at $5.8M along with AFFO per share benefit at $0.20 from the applied security deposits.

While some of the defaulted assets have been re-tenanted with the management aiming to refresh its tenant portfolio (to healthier ones) over the next 18 to 36 months, it is apparent that we are likely to see lumpy rental collection over the next few quarters, pending "the (new) tenants obtaining the requisite approvals to operate."

For now, IIPR has already strengthened its balance sheet, with $9M of its debt retired at a discount and $10M in new capital raised in the latest quarter, resulting in the healthier balance sheet with $220.8M in liquidity (-7.4% QoQ/ +8.5% YoY), significantly aided by:

  • the nearly $2.2B or 84.6% of its assets unencumbered, offering the management greater financial flexibility whenever necessary,
  • the minimal $291M debt maturing in May 2026, with future refinancing possible,
  • the highly prudent debt-to-gross assets ratio of 11%, compared to the REIT averages of 33.2%, and
  • the higher debt service coverage ratio of 16.8x, compared to the minimum REIT requirement of 1.5x.

These metrics further underscore why the management team has been competently navigating the challenging cannabis operations, the uncertain macroeconomic environment, and the structural risks from illicit players/ prolonged federal legalization.

The Consensus Forward Estimates

On the other hand, it is undeniable that IIPR's last declared dividend at $1.90 per share (inline QoQ/ YoY) has already triggered its dangerously high AFFO payout ratio of 97.9% (+12.4 points QoQ/ +12 YoY), compared to the sector median of 74.97%.

With FQ2'25 likely to bring forth a ratio at the triple digits, given the lack of security deposits, we may see the management face potential cash flow issues on its dividend payouts over the next few quarters, if the new tenants have yet to pay rents and/ or further tenant defaults occur.

Combined with the mixed consensus forward estimates through FY2026, it goes without saying that IIPR's rich dividend investment thesis remains relatively risky at current levels, despite a floor seemingly materializing at the recent April 2025 bottom of $48s ranges.

So, Is IIPR Stock A Buy, Sell, or Hold?

IIPR 6Y Stock Price

These pessimistic reasons may also be why IIPR has lost -79.7% of its value since the November 2021 top, with the stock effectively losing all of its 6Y gains albeit seemingly well-supported at the early 2019/ April 2025 bottom of $48s ranges.

The meltdown is unsurprising indeed, given the prolonged tenant renewal over the next 18 to 36 months, the uncertain cash flow risks arising from the now overly expensive dividend payouts, and the structural headwinds in the cannabis industry.

This reason is also why investors may want to temper their expectations surrounding the REIT's future dividend payouts, since a cut may occur if the headwinds discussed above do not lift in the near-term - a risk that is likely priced in the currently oversold stock prices.

IIPR Valuations

On the other hand, we believe that much of the risks have been baked into IIPR's discounted FWD Price/ AFFO valuations of 8.12x, compared to the 5Y mean of 23.70x, the 3Y pre-pandemic mean of 35.90x, and the REIT sector median of 14.65x.

These reasons are also why the REIT continues to offer an extremely rich dividend yields of 13.76%, up drastically from the 4Y averages of 7.09% and the sector median of 4.89%.

While multiple risks remain, we believe the emergence of a floor at IIPR's stock prices of $48s ranges and the FWD Price/ AFFO valuation bottom of 6.38x in April 2025 have been highly encouraging indeed, as the bulls also defend its upward recovery since then.

Based on the consensus FY2026 AFFO estimates of $7.09 and the current FWD Price/ AFFO valuations of 8.12x, it is apparent that the stock is still trading near to our updated fair value estimates of $57.50.

Based on the speculative upward re-rating in IIPR's valuations nearer to the REIT sector median of 14.65x (still cheap compared to its 5Y mean of 23.70x), there is now a near doubling upside potential to our bull-case long-term price target of $103.80 as well.

These reasons are also why we are cautiously upgrading to a Buy, with the caveat that the stock is only suitable for those with higher risk tolerance and longer investing trajectory, since it remains to be seen when the tenant renewal process may be completed and when the cannabis may be federally legalized.

IIPR now presents a contrarian Buy opportunity indeed.

data.title: Hess Midstream: Deal Or No Deal, Shareholders Win
data.description:
Investment Thesis

There is a lot of noise surrounding Hess Midstream (NYSE:HESM) and whether or not Chevron (CVX) will take it over if its acquisition of Hess (HES) is Successful. I see value in the midstream under both scenarios. I believe Chevron will try to take over the midstream, but will have to pay a significant premium; however, if Chevron's deal does not close, or it does not want to take over the midstream, it will continue to operate as usual, and my DCF model shows a slight undervaluation. Both options are beneficial for shareholders at current prices, so I am giving Hess Midstream a Buy rating.

About Hess Midstream

Hess Midstream provides midstream services, primarily to Hess Corporation's oil and gas production in North Dakota's Bakken region. Hess Midstream has long-term contracts with Hess until 2033 and minimum volume commitments (MVCs) with them until 2027, which protects their commodity risk. Their fees also adjust for the CPI data, protecting their inflation risk, and their deal also protects 80% of their current and future revenues. Although spun off in 2017, Hess Corporation and Global Infrastructure Partners (GIP) remain the majority sponsors, and, at the end of 2024, they had a 52.7% voting interest, down from 70.2% in 2023.

Insider and Institutional Transactions

I do not believe that insider and institutional activity tells the full story of a stock, but the volumes have been significant in the past twelve months, so I wanted to include it. In the past year, BlackRock has sold 27.5 million shares, or about $1.05 billion. GIP has also sold 12.65 million shares for $444 million in the past year. With almost $1.5 billion of shares being sold by two parties for a company with a market cap of about $8.1 billion is worrying about the future. That being said, there cannot be sellers without buyers. In 2024, Hess Midstream repurchased about $300 million of its stock, partially offsetting the selling. Alps Advisors has purchased an additional 12.44 million shares of Hess Midstream since Q1 2024, bringing their total to 26.36 million shares. Additionally, Blackstone has purchased about 5.55 million shares since Q1 2024, but importantly, Blackstone purchased 4.3 million shares in Q1 2025, bringing their total position to 6.17 million shares. This abnormally large addition in Q1 2025 represents a purchase of about 5.66% of the daily volume (4.3 million shares / 76 shares traded in Q1 2025). If we add Alps's addition in Q1 2025 of 6.25 million shares, Blackstone and Alps purchases represent about 13.88% of the daily volume. I see GIP and BlackRock as inevitable exits, but the large purchases from Blackstone and Alps are encouraging. I am interested to see how these firms behave in Q2 2025, but I see this as a positive sign for the company at a time with high levels of uncertainty.

Chevron's Acquisition of Hess and What It Means for Hess Midstream

In October 2023, Chevron announced an all-stock acquisition of Hess. Chevron is very interested in Hess' Guyana assets due to their long-term production growth and high cash margins, and Chevron is also looking to expand its US domestic production, and it sees value in Hess' Bakken assets as they can connect with Chevron's DJ and Permian Basin assets in Colorado and Texas and New Mexico, respectively. There remain some doubts over the deal closure, due to Exxon and CNOOC claiming they have a right of first refusal to purchase Hess' stake in Guyana. Per the deal, Hess shareholders receive 1.025 shares of Chevron for each Hess share. On Friday, June 27th, Hess shares closed at $138.97, and Chevron's shares closed at $143.79. At Chevron's share price of $143.79, Hess should trade at $147.38 vs Hess' share price of $138.97. There is a 6% arbitrage spread between the two, so the market is anticipating that the deal is likely to close, but there is still some risk that Exxon and CNOOC are successful in stopping, delaying, or restructuring the deal (more clarity expected in Q3 2025). I will address the possible outcomes for Hess Midstream as a result of the Hess and Chevron deal closing in its current form and not closing in the next section.

Possibility 1: Hess Deal Closes

I believe Chevron will likely restructure some of Hess's operations and could look into acquiring the midstream. Hess currently has four rigs running in the Bakken region and plans to remain with four in 2025 (which Hess midstream exclusively supports until 2033 per their contract with Hess), but Bakken E&P is not profitable. In 2024, Hess' United States E&P segment lost $497 million, vs. a loss of $291 million in 2023. That loss includes $1.376 billion and $1.245 billion of midstream tariffs in 2024 and 2023, respectively, and various other higher costs relative to the international segment.

While the United States E&P segment produces a loss on the surface, the midstream expense goes to Hess Midstream, for which Hess has an ownership stake, so I am not overly concerned about Chevron reducing production in the Bakken, given that they would inherit Hess' 38% consolidated ownership interest in Hess Midstream.

Although Chevron could divest Hess' Bakken assets (more on this later), I believe they will continue to run them, and they will most likely purchase Hess Midstream. Historically, Chevron has acquired other E&P companies and their midstream assets. For example, in 2020, Chevron announced an agreement to acquire Noble Energy. Noble Energy owned a majority stake (60%) in its midstream company, and Chevron purchased the remaining stock in the midstream in March 2021 at a slight discount to the market price.

If Chevron's deal closes, I would predict that Chevron would keep Hess' Bakken assets and attempt to take control of Hess Midstream; however, I would expect a premium to be paid for Hess Midstream. Chevron would at most own approximately 38% of the class A shares (assuming they convert all their class B shares into class A), which is not a majority stake as with the Noble deal. With large firms like the Alps and Blackstone owning approximately 15% (32.53 million shares between them / 219 million diluted shares outstanding) of the shares with the ability to purchase more (especially given a price decrease from their last purchases), it will be harder for Chevron to avoid paying a premium for the midstream. To make matters worse for Chevron, Hess Midstream announced it would be repurchasing $190 million of its Class B stock (which Hess and the other sponsors own) or about 2.4% of the consolidated company. According to the report, the public will now own 55.1%, GIP will own 7.1%, and Hess Corporation will own 37.8% of the midstream. I believe that Chevron will want to vertically integrate itself by acquiring the midstream, but it will need to pay a premium for the assets due to the more complex ownership structure of Hess Midstream and current market conditions. If the deal closes, I would purchase the midstream stock (with a current dividend yield of 7.17% and constant share repurchases) and wait for Chevron to acquire it at a premium to the market price.

Possibility 2: Hess Deal Does Not Close or Chevron Divests Bakken Assets

It is possible that Exxon and CNOOC win the case, and the deal does not close, or Chevron decides to divest Hess' Bakken assets. In this scenario, I am more focused on the future of production in the region, so I can estimate Hess Midstream's cash flows in the future. Below are Hess' proven developed and undeveloped reserves.

I am focused on the United States segment, as that is where Hess Midstream operates. Hess has two main assets in the United States Category, the Gulf of America and the Bakken. In 2024, Bakken production averaged about 204k Barrels of Oil Equivalent (boe) per day and about 30k boe per day in the Gulf of America. Although Hess does not disclose reserves by Gulf of America and Bakken, there are 928 million undeveloped boe in the United States that Hess has access to. I will use 2024's production ratio to estimate proven Bakken reserves for Hess. I estimate 809 million proven boe in the Bakken. That being said, Hess has increased their proven reserves, and to forecast this, I will use a reverse replacement ratio. I estimate that in 2024, the reverse replacement ratio was 33% (increase in Bakken reserves / Bakken production). I performed this calculation from 2019 to 2024 with an average reserve replacement ratio of 25%. Using a 25% reserve replacement ratio, for the duration of the contract and a 0% reserve replacement ratio for the years following (due to no additional investment from Hess and assuming no contract extension), I project Hess Midstream will service 1 billion boe in the future (already served 460 million boe).

Now let's turn boe into numbers. Hess Midstream's long-term projections are below.

EBITDA grows faster than volume due to fees being linked to the CPI. I project these CPI adjustments to increase revenue by 2% each year, with other revenue increases being from volume increases. I assume volumes will operate above the MVC, and revenue will solely be based on volume and CPI adjustments. Over the lifetime of the contract, I project Hess to service about 800 million boe. With volumes increasing at an average rate of 2.5% per year after 2026. To turn volumes into numbers, I will use an EBITDA to boepd ratio. In 2024, this ratio was $0.0056, and I expect this to increase by 2% each year based on CPI adjustments after 2026. My contract EBITDA, volume, and reserve projections are shown below.

After the contract, I assume Hess will scale down its operations, and HESM's EBITDA/Volume ratio will stay constant at $0.0067. My projections are shown below.

Now let's turn EBITDA into a discounted cash flow model. I project a cost of capital of 6.64% including a cost of equity of 7.57% comprising an industry beta of .72, risk risk-free rate of 4.41%, and an equity risk premium of 4.41%. My cost of debt is 6% from the historical interest expense/debt ratios. I project Hess will need to deploy additional capital in the next few years to service undeveloped reserves, then it will just need to maintain its asset base. Additionally, I project Hess Midstream will liquidate its assets at the end of the non-contract period with a discount of 20% to pay off its debt and return capital to shareholders. My full DCF model is shown below.

Based on my calculations, Hess Midstream is currently trading slightly below its intrinsic value, but this value could increase significantly if the Hess contract is extended and they or another owner continue investing in the Bakken region.

Final Thoughts and Risks

Hess Midstream finds itself at a crossroads: Chevron will acquire it, or it will continue to be publicly traded. If Chevron wants to vertically integrate itself, I believe it will have to pay a premium for the midstream, unlike its Noble Energy deals. Chevron would not have the majority of the shares in the midstream and would need to make an offer above the market price or buy shares on the open market, both of which would cause the share price to increase.

If Chevron decides to sell Hess' Bakken assets or if the Hess-Chevron deal fails to close, it should be business as usual for the midstream. In this scenario, I conservatively found that the midstream is slightly undervalued.

I see two main risks: commodity prices and legal. While Hess Midstream's revenues are not fully dependent on commodity prices, Hess or another owner will only continue operating in the region if the economics are right, and even if there can be 1 billion proven boe in the region, lower commodity prices would make Hess or another owner less likely to use all the reserves and would make cashflows dependent on MVCs. Secondly, Hess pays high fees for its midstream services directly, as evidenced by Hess Midstream's significantly higher gross, EBITDA, and EBIT margins compared to its peers, but Hess earns a return through its 38% ownership share in the midstream. That said, Continental Resources has filed a lawsuit against Hess. This lawsuit is targeted at Hess and not Hess Midstream, but the results could affect the fees Hess Midstream receives.

Absent these major risks significantly changing the business, I believe shareholders will see a good return whether or not Chevron takes over the midstream, resulting in a Buy rating at current prices.

data.title: Dividend Harvesting Portfolio Week 226: $22,600 Allocated, $2,367.69 In Projected Dividends
data.description:
This was a monumental week as the S&P 500 closed at all-time highs after appreciating by 2.16% over the past week. The Nasdaq also gained 2.61% this week as big tech drove the market higher. The U.S. conducted an airstrike over the weekend on Iranian nuclear sites. The aftermath was a fragile ceasefire between Israel and Iran, oil sharply declining, which alleviated inflationary pressures supporting market sentiment, and fears of a broader Middle East conflict subsided. The June FOMC meeting suggested potential rate reductions and lower Treasury yields. A lot has happened since Liberation Day as the S&P slipped into a bear market, falling 21.31% (1,309.11) from 6,144.15 in the middle of February to 4,835.04 at the beginning of April. The bear market was short-lived as this dip was gobbled up just like every other dip, as the S&P 500 rebounded by 28.03% (1,355.05) off the lows to 6,190.09. Things move quickly as July 4th weekend is next week, then we roll into earnings season with JPMorgan Chase (JPM) reporting Q2 results on 7/15. I think that corporate earnings will be relatively strong, financials will set the tone, and big-tech will lead the market higher into the end of the summer. I am excited to continue building out the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio and believe that many of the positions I have been allocating capital to will do particularly well in the future, especially when the Fed pivots on monetary policy.

The Dividend Harvesting Portfolio hit a new milestone this week in profitability. After allocating $22,600 to the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio, the account balance finished week 226 at $28,718.15. The account's profitability increased ($141.16) to $6,118.15, which is a return on invested capital of 27.07%. This week, I added to my positions in AGNC Investment Corp. (AGNC), Truist Financial Corporation (TFC), and Bristol-Myers Squibb (BMY). The Dividend Harvesting Portfolio generated $39.11 of dividend income in week 226. The combination of reinvesting the generated dividend income and making these investments increased the forward projected annualized dividend income by $5.80 (0.26%) to $2,361. The Dividend Harvesting Portfolio has generated $4,244.75 of income since inception, which is 18.78% of the total capital I have allocated toward the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio. I think that the Fed is going to wait until September at this point, as they will likely remain data dependent. While I disagree with their decision and believe they should have started cutting in May, I am looking forward to earnings season and being able to add to positions that will benefit from rate cuts. I believe that the ETFs and CEFs that have large technology exposure will do very well over the summer, and that the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio will continue to do well as the months progress.

The Overall Performance of the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio

A new milestone was reached, and The Dividend Harvesting Portfolio reached $6,118.15 of profitability. The previous record was in week 209 when profitability reached $6,106.71. This portfolio was started from the ground up with $100. I have been documenting how I would build a dividend portfolio on a fixed budget of $100 per week, with the goals of generating recurring income and mitigating downside risk. Capital appreciation has always been a secondary goal, as my main focus is around stability and income. I don't allow positions to exceed 5% or sectors to exceed 20%. The portfolio is very diversified and is generating $2,367.69 in dividend income, which is an 8.24% portfolio yield and a 10.48% yield on cost. I am looking forward to seeing how the portfolio progresses, as there is a good amount of dividend income being reinvested each week.

The Dividend Harvesting Portfolio Dividend Section

Here's how much dividend income is generated per investment basket:

  • ETFs $776.49 (32.80%)
  • Equities $563.40 (23.80%)
  • CEFs $400.49 (16.91%)
  • REITs $383.71 (16.21%)
  • BDCs $243.60 (10.29%)

Collecting dividends can serve many functions in a portfolio. Some investors utilize dividends to supplement their income and live off of them. I'm building a dividend portfolio for myself 30 years into the future. In 2022, I collected $507.80 in dividend income from 533 dividends. In 2023, I collected $978.11 in dividend income from 660 dividends. 2024 was a great year as the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio generated $1,623.14 in dividend income from 709 dividends. After 26 weeks in 2025, $1,065.37 of dividend income was produced, which is 65.64% of the total income from 2024. There were 363 positions that paid a dividend, which is 51.20% of the dividends generated in 2024.

There was $39.11 of dividend income generated in week 26, which brought the amount of dividend income I have generated on a weekly basis to $40.98. This is an increase of 31.27% ($9.76) compared to 2024's weekly average of $31.21. 2025 continues to look much different than 2024, and June still has 1 more trading day in it. Half of the year is basically over, and I have generated over 65% of 2024's dividend income already. I think that the second half of 2025 is going to be very strong for the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio, as all of the income I have added will continue to compound as I continue building out the portfolio. I think that I may finish the year exceeding $2,600 of forward dividend income.

June has 1 more trading day, and I am a bit surprised that only $129.95 of dividend income has been generated. It's going to be interesting to see how much dividend income is generated on June 30th, as the 3rd month in Q1 ended with $189.26 of dividend income. I have a suspicion that a bunch of positions are paying their dividends on June 30th, and the dividend income for the month of June is going to jump much higher. Once June is over, I will revise my projections, but I have a feeling that I will exceed $200 in dividend income during June or July and start setting new trends going forward.

One of my long-term goals is to have 75% of the positions in the portfolio generating at least 1 share per year from their dividends. There are 44 positions that are currently accomplishing this goal, and the additional dividend income being generated is $359.21. There are a lot of companies I am trying to add to, and I would like to have another 5–10 positions cross over into the green section of the table below before the end of the year. I am excited to see how this develops, as there are many companies I am eager to add to my portfolio, even as the market approaches all-time highs.

The Dividend Harvesting Portfolio Composition

It's very hard not to allocate capital toward REITs or ETFs, and these sectors will likely battle each week for the largest sector in the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio. I think there are a lot of opportunities across the REIT sector, especially since the Fed continues to stall its monetary pivot. Individual equities now represent 38.96% of the Dividend Harvesting portfolio while generating 23.80% of the dividend income. REITs, ETFs, CEFs, and BDCs make up 61.04% of the portfolio and generate 76.20% of the forward income. I am working on getting individual equities to represent more of the portfolio. The long-term goal is to have the sectors be close to equal weight, but that may not happen for 5–10 years at this rate, if ever. I have been adding to energy companies as I believe there is a big opportunity there, and I will probably continue allocating capital toward energy positions as time progresses.

Altria Group (MO) is back under 4.5% of the portfolio, but it's incredible that almost a year and a half has gone by since I added to this position, and it still represents this much of the portfolio. Verizon (VZ) has been under 3% for a while, and I would like to see Enbridge (ENB) and Realty Income (O) fall below 3% by the end of the summer if possible. The portfolio has rounded out well, excluding the 3 largest positions, and I am happy with how my asset mix is maturing. My goal is to have all of them represent less than 3.5% of the portfolio. I think that the top-20 positions are going to change a bit over the next several months, as I see a bunch of opportunities I am looking to allocate capital toward the long-term goal.

Not much changed this week as the top-10 positions remained the same, they just changed positions since last week. The NEOS Nasdaq 100 High Income ETF generated a dividend this week. My allocation to the top 10 positions is $6,153.95, and they finished the week with a value of $8,462.88. This is an ROI of $2,308.93 (37.52%). There has been $1,043.41 of dividends generated and reinvested, which is 16.96% of the initial investment into these positions. I'm now projecting that $599.73 of dividend income will be generated from the top 10 holdings, which have a forward yield of 9.75%. These positions represent 29.47% of the portfolio, while their forward dividend income is projected to generate 25.33% of the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio's annualized income.

Week 226 Additions

In week 226, I added to my positions in AGNC Investment Corp, Truist Financial Corporation, and Bristol-Myers Squibb.

AGNC Investment Corp

  • I recently wrote an article on AGNC discussing my investment thesis (can be read here)
  • AGNC has a dividend yield of 15.42% and the dividend is a compounding monster as it's paid on a monthly basis
  • AGNC has delivered 246.8% of its initial share price in dividends over 17 years, which has allowed investors to focus on the dividends and not care about what the share price is doing

Truist Financial Corporation

  • TFC has a yield that exceeds 5%, and I am trying to add more shares before their Q2 earnings
  • I believe that the regional banks will do very well when the Fed pivots, and I am trying to get more exposure to TFC
  • When the Fed starts cutting rates, I expect TFC's loan originations to increase, which could increase their forward EPS

Bristol-Myers Squibb

  • I am trying to get BMY to generate at least 1 share from their dividends, and I need to add more to this position
  • BMY has a yield of 5.22% which is very strong in the pharmaceutical field
  • I think BMY is undervalued as it trades for under 7 times 2025 earnings and generates around $13 billion in free cash flow (FCF)

Week 227 Game Plan

In week 227, I am considering adding to Kinder Morgan (KMI), and the JPMorgan Nasdaq Equity Premium Income ETF (JEPQ) heading into earnings season

Conclusion

The first half of 2025 is almost over, and the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio is doing exceptionally well. I will be revising my initial projections for the year, as I may have been too conservative. This portfolio was started with $100, and I believe I have shown that if you're willing to do the work, you can build a strong income-producing portfolio over time on any budget. From $100 the account now has $28,718.15 in it while generating $2,367.69 of forward dividend income. I think what is even crazier is that the projected monthly dividend income is $197.31 and the weekly projected dividend income is $45.53, which is almost half of the weekly ongoing investment. I'm excited about the future as I believe the market will do well, The Dividend Harvesting Portfolio will do well, and the amount of income being generated will reach $5,200 quicker than I had expected. Please leave all of your suggestions in the comment section, as I look forward to interacting with everyone.

data.title: GPIQ: It Might Be Just What You Need For The Rest Of 2025
data.description:
Introduction

Recently, I have been looking at the market opportunities in some of the well-known technology companies, with stock prices still deep in the red for the year. On the other hand, I don't feel totally comfortable adding more growth exposures as we go into the traditionally slow months of the year. Goldman Sachs Nasdaq-100 Premium Income ETF (NASDAQ:GPIQ) appears to be a better play for me due to its high income distribution and the portfolio strategy that makes it a good growth investment consideration for 2025.

GPIQ overview

GPIQ is Goldman Sachs's solution for growth income derived from writing covered calls off the Nasdaq-100 index portfolio QQQ. On the surface, GPIQ looks very much like many similar income ETFs, including the JPMorgan Nasdaq Equity Premium Income ETF (JEPQ), which I covered early in 2025, and more recently, First Trust Nasdaq BuyWrite Income ETF (FTQI). They may offer a double-digit yield in monthly distribution and a relatively low expense ratio when compared to their CEF peers. Investors may notice small differences in the distribution rates and the expenses when assuming a similar underlying portfolio and consistent income sources (premiums from selling heavily traded covered calls). The following are the top 10 holdings for GPIQ and QQQ, both are heavy in the same top 10, with over 50% weights.

However, the key difference, which could be "hidden" inside the official documents of these ETFs, is the way the ETFs manage the options, including the call strike target and the actual option selling percentage. The ETF performance behavior and performance are dictated by these option trading strategies. GPIQ mainly differs from the other ETFs in managing the selling option coverage (in percentage). In other words, selling 50% covered calls would allow better price appreciation than 100% call selling. That's why GPIQ:

Dynamically sells call options, allowing for participation with rising markets and potential outperformance in negative to flat markets.

As a result, GPIQ could outperform the S&P 500 Index and other larger income ETFs, such as JEPQ, in total returns (a performance chart is given in the later section).

The ETF has gained popularity in a relatively short history since its start on 10/24/2023. In less than two years, GPIQ has accumulated an AUM of over $1 billion, which is impressive considering there are many similar offerings in the market. GPIQ currently has a monthly distribution with a dividend rate of over 10.13% lower than JEPQ's 11.35%, and an expense ratio of 0.29%, which is also lower than JEPQ's 0.35%.

Some key tech components will catch up

The growth-centric QQQ is breaking new highs in recent days. However, many other investors may not be entirely happy as some key technology companies are still some distance away from reaching the previous all-time high achieved early in 2025. Apple Inc. (AAPL) is one of such companies, and its stock is about 15% lower than its 2025 high and about 20% lower than its all-time high accomplished near the end of 2024.

However, the recent technical analysis on AAPL shows a promising price setup (above 50-day and 20-day moving averages), which could lead to a breakout on the upside. Since AAPL has over 7% weight in QQQ (and GPIQ), it would likely help keep the uptrend of QQQ. Similarly, the other heavyweights in QQQ, like Amazon and Google, also have some room to grow and reach the old record highs. These companies together have a huge market cap of $10 trillion, so they would push/keep up the growth sector and GPIQ/QQQ in a very meaningful way.

Earning growth will continue to build on top of the upcoming Q2 reports

Looking at the next tier in the growth space, the current market bull run in the last month seems to hide away the fact that many growth companies are still struggling to recover from the deep price loss incurred in 2025. The following is a partial list I have compiled using the data available in SA. Notice that these companies are the key components of the GPIQ portfolio.

Most of these companies are in negative YTD price performance, and all of them are yet to make the previous highs reached earlier this year. Notice a couple of them have still been down in double digits in 2025.

On the other hand, the forward revenue is projected in the high-growth trajectory as shown in the above table. Many are growing revenue at over 20% for 2025. I would pay close attention to these companies in the coming earnings season. Any positive news could trigger a jump in stock price and result in a significant (double-digit) price gain in a relatively short time. I believe these potential opportunities would not require too much help from a specific catalyst or a unique tailwind.

The main takeaway here is that I expect these companies to carry on bullish momentum and drive a decent performance for QPIQ's underlying portfolio in 2025.

GPIQ is a well-balanced play for growth income investors

Looking ahead, the next few months may not be a traditionally strong season for the equity market, including QQQ. As highlighted in the following table, QQQ has not been doing so well in August, September, and October. The 10-year averages are mostly lower than other months, particularly the earlier months of the year.

Given such historical patterns, it might be a better strategy to hold GPIQ instead of QQQ. Investors may remain bullish on the growth stocks for the rest of 2025, but will likely have to face a bumpy road first.

There are two main reasons in favor of GPIQ. The first is that the seasonality of the above QQQ price patterns typically means high volatility; GPIQ has a much lower volatility at the portfolio level. GPIQ's 52-week Historical Volatility is 20.3, and QQQ's is at 22. This means GPIQ will offer more stability.

The second reason is that investors have the flexibility to deploy the high-income distribution monthly. For example, one can use it to buy QQQ if the volatility turns out to be low or acceptable for a certain time. This gradual approach is my favorite one to navigate through the market volatility. Keep in mind that the flexibility also applies to buying shares of other choices of interests, such as small-cap stocks, value plays for market rotation, or just more income-generating holdings, such as reinvesting in GPIQ.

The following is a 1-year total performance comparison for the mentioned growth ETFs. One can see GPIQ is a rather "surprising" winner by gaining the most, 13.37% beating QQQ (13.18%) and S&P 500 SPY (12.35%).

Note that the above time frame was specifically chosen to illustrate the GPIQ's potential to outperform during certain choppy market periods. I think the upcoming months may exhibit the choppiness that could enable GPIQ to do better or reasonably well.

Risk analysis

All the risks pertinent to the growth portfolio of QQQ will be present in GPIQ, although some impacts could be smaller, such as slightly downward market behavior, which may be caused by macro worries, like slowing economic growth, earnings growth deceleration, etc.

However, the main risk is from the events that could cause sharp drawdowns quickly. For example, the recent tariff-related news could send the market to have big drops in the past 3 months or so. I would expect much less dramatic events to break out on the tariff front, but they are still possible in the next few weeks or months. Big drawdowns are typically very difficult and take a long time to recover from for GPIQ.

Closing thoughts

The stock market may take a breather moving forward into the next few months that may not be too market-friendly, historically speaking, but income investors could still hold on to a growth income portfolio such as GPIQ. Some key technology growth names are still in the recovery process, and the overall growth segment is expected to remain resilient. If the coming (Q2) earnings from technology companies would bring more positive "surprises", the stock market could continue its uptrend in the 2nd half of the year. In such a case, QPIQ has a great chance to outperform the S&P 500. I rate GPIQ a buy and recommend it as a solid growth play for 2025.

data.title: SPHD Explained: Is This High-Yielding Dividend ETF Right For You?
data.description:
Introduction

The Invesco S&P 500 High Dividend Low Volatility ETF (NYSEARCA:SPHD) is designed to track the 50 least volatile high-yielding S&P 500 Index stocks. Aimed at investors who prioritize safety and income over growth, SPHD has a 0.30% expense ratio and has amassed $3.16 billion in assets under management since its launch in 2012, making it one of the more recognizable ETFs on the market today. In this article, I will walk you through SPHD's selection process, the types of returns you can expect in bull and bear markets, how reinvesting impacts long-term portfolio income, and compare and contrast its fundamentals against two cap-weighted and equal-weighted S&P 500 Index ETFs. I hope you enjoy the read.

The Invesco S&P 500 High Dividend Low Volatility ETF: An Overview

Product Details and Fast Facts

Consider the following product overview provided by Invesco:

The Invesco S&P 500 High Dividend Low Volatility ETF is based on the S&P 500 Low Volatility High Dividend Index. The Fund will invest at least 90% of its total assets in common stocks that comprise the Index. Standard & Poor's compiles, maintains and calculates the Index, which is composed of 50 securities traded on the S&P 500 Index that historically have provided high dividend yields and low volatility. The Fund and the Index are rebalanced and reconstituted semi-annually, in January and July.

Additional characteristics, as described on SPHD's website, fact sheet, and summary prospectus, are as follows:

  • Fund Ticker: SPHD
  • Index Provider: S&P Dow Jones Indices LLC
  • Exchange: NYSE Arca
  • Inception Date: October 18, 2012
  • Total Expense Ratio: 0.30%
  • Last Price: $48.37
  • 12 Month Distribution Yield: 3.48%
  • Bid/Ask Premium/Discount: -0.04%
  • Holdings (Excluding Cash): 50
  • Average Trading Volume (30 Days): 519,944
  • Shares Outstanding: 66.47M
  • Assets Under Management: $3.16B
  • Weighting Scheme: Yield-Weighted
  • Marginable: Yes
  • Short Selling: Yes
  • Options: Yes

SPHD Index Methodology

As noted in the graphic below, the S&P 500 Low Volatility High Dividend Index "selects the 50 least volatile stocks (measured by trailing 252-day standard deviation) from the 75 highest yielding stocks of the S&P 500." The chart to the right shows how SPHD outperformed the S&P 500 Index in 8/11 of the largest drawdowns since its inception, and this captures the essence of what SPHD tries to accomplish. It's designed for investors averse to risk and who desire an above-average income.

There are a couple of key points I want to highlight. First, the number of stocks per GICS sector is capped at 10 at each reconstitution after the last business day in January and July and at each rebalancing after the last business day of December and June. Second, dividend yield is the driving factor, not low volatility. It's used in the initial step to bring the number of stocks from 500 to 75, and again in the last step, when Index components are weighted by their trailing dividend yields.

To help put all these steps together, I created a graph showing how the S&P 500 Index breaks down when you filter for the top 300, 225, 150, and 75 stocks by dividend yield. I also calculated each stock's 252-day trailing volatility and provided the median figure for all S&P 500 stocks by sector, so you can get a feel for how that last volatility screen impacts the Index.

The bottom numbers show that there are currently 17 Real Estate securities within the top 75 by yield, followed by 14 and 11 for Consumer Staples and Financials. However, the 10 security per sector rule applies, so next in line are Utilities, Energy, and Health Care, which have 24, 15, and 12 securities within the top 150. That 150 line is a reasonable cutoff for the foreseeable future, and it points to the Index always favoring these sectors. Not surprisingly, these were also SPHD's top six sectors as of June 27, 2025.

Notice how Technology is absent from the sector holdings list, and that will likely be the case moving forward. Only four stocks (HPQ, SWKS, HPE, TXN) rank in the top 150 by yield, and all of them rank outside the top 300 by volatility, which is what disqualified HPQ and SWKS. As for HPE and TXN, they'll need a significant change in their yield and volatility to have a chance. Given how those two statistics often work against each other, it's likely SPHD's tech sector exposure will be limited to only two stocks at most, or less than 3% by weight.

SPHD Performance and Dividends

Annualized Active Return vs. S&P 500 Index ETF
