The following table highlights SPHD's annualized active returns against the iShares Core S&P 500 ETF (IVV). For the first eight years, the active returns were relatively modest and mostly around the +/- 10% level. However, active returns have grown substantially since the pandemic, and except for 2022, in a negative way. The expansion of big tech really hurt SPHD shareholders from a total returns perspective.

This change shows up nicely on SPHD's three-year rolling returns chart. After trading places with IVV for the first half, SPHD began to underperform in 2018 before bottoming out in March 2020. Performance improved due to its 18.73% beat in 2022, but SPHD's three-year returns have still trailed IVV's for the last seven years or so.

Dividends and Dividend Growth

Although SPHD has underperformed on total returns, it has proven to be a reliable income investment. For example, a person with $10,000 choosing to reinvest all dividends would grow their annual income from $436 to $1,068 between 2013 and 2024, which works out to 8.49% compound annual growth over 11 years. Meanwhile, an IVV shareholder would see their income grow from $235 to $661 over that period, or an annualized 9.86%.

IVV's dividend growth rate is better, but SPHD's higher starting yield allows investors to benefit more from compounding. From the chart, the difference in income was $201 in 2013 ($436 vs. $235), but SPHD's income grew to $1,068 in 2024, a difference of $407.

However, this income growth advantage was not present for investors choosing to withdraw their distributions. As shown below, SPHD's annual income was $195 higher in 2013 ($428 vs. $233) but only $141 higher in 2024 ($675 vs. $534), demonstrating how dividend growth and total returns are closely linked. IVV narrowed the gap due to stronger price appreciation, which increased the portfolio base from which dividends are paid. Consequently, SPHD was less attractive for income investors as the holding period extended.

SPHD Tax Considerations

Readers should also consider asset allocation if they plan to invest in SPHD. Given its favoritism for REITs, not all of the fund's distributions will be considered qualified dividend income, or QDI. You can see SPHD's QDI percentage in the fund's latest Annual Financial Statements, and for the fiscal year ending August 31, 2024, it was 84%.

With 20.36% currently allocated to the Real Estate sector, all of which is to REITs, investors can expect SPHD's QDI to drift closer to 80% in the short term. In addition, with 17 and 24 REITs currently in the top 75 and top 150 by yield, respectively, there's a good chance SPHD's QDI will be in the 80-90% range for the foreseeable future, even if the sector recovers. Therefore, I suggest holding SPHD in a tax-advantaged account like an IRA or a 401 (k).

Fundamentals: SPHD vs. S&P 500 Index ETFs

Big tech's success has also created significant fundamental differences between SPHD and S&P 500 Index ETFs like IVV. As shown below, SPHD's forward P/E ratio is 15.08x, compared to 22.33 for the S&P 500 Index. SPHD also has a much higher dividend yield (5.36% vs. 1.48%), but its growth rates are several percentage points worse across the board.

The chart to the left also confirms SPHD has strong value, dividend, low volatility, and size factor exposure compared to the S&P 500 Index, with offsets in growth, momentum, and quality. Besides performance history, this is a different way to view ETF investing. Readers may consider using SPHD to diversify their portfolios from a factor perspective and cover their bases in case the big tech stocks that lead the S&P 500 Index fall out of favor.

I also created a fundamental statistics table for SPHD's top 25 holdings using data from Seeking Alpha. At the bottom are two comparators: IVV and an equal-weight S&P 500 Index fund, which is essentially the Invesco S&P 500 Equal Weight ETF (RSP) with a forced rebalance to 0.20% per stock. While the holdings are bound to change, the factor differences paint a nice picture of what SPHD attempts to accomplish. Have a quick scan, and I'll provide some high-level takeaways afterward.

1. Nearly all of SPHD's top 25 holdings have trailing dividend yields above 3%, and its weighted average dividend yield, otherwise known as the Index yield, is strong at 5.11%. In comparison, the Index yields for IVV and RSP (after a forced rebalance) are only 1.23% and 1.89%.

2. SPHD's weighted average 252-day standard deviation of returns is 23.42% compared to 31.29% and 29.20% for IVV and RSP, respectively. While SPHD and RSP are similar in terms of size factor exposure (weighted average market capture), SPHD does a much better job selecting low-volatility stocks. That's partially attributed to its volatility screen, but it has more to do with the sectors SPHD is designed to favor the most, like Real Estate, Consumer Staples, and Utilities. These sectors are already considered less volatile on average, so in this sense, the Index's volatility screen may be a bit redundant.

3. SPHD trades at 17.92x trailing earnings, which represents a 32% and 17% discount relative to IVV and RSP. SPHD also trades at a 50% discount relative to IVV when using the P/CF ratio, so this is why Morningstar's Factor Profile shows SPHD with extreme value factor exposure. That's also complemented by poor growth factor exposure, evidenced by SPHD's -0.86% three-year historical earnings per share growth rate. Sales growth isn't much better at 3.86%, and that pales in comparison to IVV's 11.07% figure.

4. SPHD's weighted average ROE, ROA, and net margins are 17%, 10%, and 11% lower than IVV's, indicating its components are of lesser quality. That's an important takeaway for all high-yield investors, as deeply discounted high-yield stocks might be trading low for good reasons. It's important to do proper due diligence and be prepared to sacrifice capital appreciation in exchange for a higher income.

Summary and Conclusion

SPHD may be appropriate for conservative equity investors seeking an above-average income. Its current annual distribution rate is 3.51%, paid quarterly, and since yield and price are inversely correlated, it tends to select S&P 500 Index stocks on the lower end of the market cap spectrum. In particular, my deeper analysis of the Index's composition reveals that SPHD will likely favor the Real Estate, Consumer Staples, and Utilities sectors for the foreseeable future, three areas underrepresented in S&P 500 Index ETFs. That means you can add diversification to the list of benefits SPHD offers, alongside lower volatility and higher income.

It may also be useful to view SPHD's advantages from a factor perspective. Its selection process indicates it should consistently do a good job emphasizing the value, dividend, and low volatility factors, while providing minimal exposure to the growth, quality, and, at least temporarily, momentum factors. My own fundamental observations indicate this divide has increased since the pandemic, leading to large return differences. As a result, SPHD shareholders should be prepared for relatively poor total returns in exchange for the higher distributions, which can be accelerated through reinvestment.

This article answers these three main questions about SPHD:

  1. What type of investor is SPHD best suited for?
  2. How can investors utilize SPHD as part of a broader investment strategy?
  3. What types of market conditions does SPHD tend to succeed in?

I hope I have answered these questions to your satisfaction, but if anything is unclear, please feel free to reach out in the comments section below. I should also add that this article was intended to provide a general overview of SPHD and, as such, does not offer an investment opinion about the ETF. That said, I am happy to provide my opinion on other funds that interest you and compare SPHD's fundamentals and performance with competing income investments. Thank you for reading, and I look forward to the discussion below.

Editor's note: This article is intended to provide a general overview of the ETF for education purposes only and, unlike other articles on Seeking Alpha, does not offer an investment opinion about the ETF.

data.title: Allegion: Continued M&A Efforts Add To Its Track Record
data.description:
Towards the end of 2024, I concluded that I was not locked into shares of Allegion (NYSE:ALLE), despite a well-timed purchase of the security assets of Stanley Black & Decker (SWK) back in 2022, followed by bolt-on acquisitions made in the meantime.

Trading at a reasonable high-teens earnings multiple, amidst a reasonable amount of debt taken on, I believed that shares traded at largely fair valuations, with these being fair valuations, not necessary being appealing valuations.

In the meantime, shares have been trading flat amidst continued bolt-on M&A efforts which add to the track record here, as shares look largely fairly valued here, as I am growing more impressed with the business here.

Security Products

Allegion is a pure-play provider of security and access solutions, with the business having a mission to create a safer and more accessible world. The company employs over 12,000 employees which develop, manufacture and sell over 30 trusted brands, active across 25 countries, with the business supported by over a thousand patents. Brands include names like AXA, Bricard, Kryptonite, Schlage, and others.

The company generated nearly $4 billion in sales in 2024, some $3.8 billion to be more precise, accompanied by steep EBITDA margins equal to nearly 25% of sales. Some two-thirds of sales are generated from mechanical solutions, about a quarter from electronic security solutions, with software & services making up the remaining 7% of sales. The business is tied to the Americas in which 80% of sales are generated, with 20% of sales derived from various international markets, which are lagging substantially in terms of margins.

The company targets impressive 7% revenue growth across the cycle, composed out of an estimated 3% M&A contribution and the remainder being the outcome of volume and pricing. Typical products to think of include door locks, e-locks, touchless actuators, closers, automatic doors, frames, hinges, etc. Notably on the electronic front, Allegion is growing its exposure, driven by M&A efforts.

The company has come a long way, having grown the business from $2.1 billion in 2015 to $3.8 billion in 2024, with revenues having nearly doubled over this period of time. Growth, to an important extent, was driven by a $900 million deal to acquire the Access Technology business from Stanley Black & Decker, adding $340 million in sales.

Operating margins near 20% have risen a bit to levels in the low-twenties with the passage of time, driven by sale and efficiency advancements, as the company announced substantial growth while buying back about one in every ten shares as well.

All this has been reflected in the share price, which has gradually risen from levels around the $60 mark in 2015 to hit a high around $150 in 2021. Ever since, shares have largely traded in a $100-$150 range, now trading at the higher end of the range.

Valuation Thoughts

For the year 2023, the company posted adjusted earnings of $6.69 per share on $3.65 billion in sales. The company guided for sales up around 2.5%, with adjusted earnings seen moving up to $7.00-$7.15 per share.

Through 2024 the company announced a couple of bolt-on transactions, as growth was not necessarily convincing. Trading around the $140 mark in December, the 88 million shares of the company were valued at $12.2 billion, this excluding $1.5 billion in net debt, for a $13.7 billion enterprise valuation.

The business was on track to post earnings close to $7.40 per share, for an 18-19 times adjusted earnings multiple, as net debt of $1.52 billion resulted in a 1.7 times leverage ratio based on EBITDA seen around $900 million. With shares having enjoyed quite some momentum in the fall, this made me require seeing a dip before getting upbeat on the shares.

Trading Flat

Since the end of 2024, shares of Allegion have traded in a $120-$150 range, with the lows induced by the tariff discussion, as the company has made a full recovery ever since.

So far this year, the company announced numerous bolt-on deals as the company announced a solid 6% increase in the quarterly dividend payable at $2.04 per share, with dividends now hiked for 11 years in a row.

In February, the company announced a 3% increase in full-year sales to $3.77 billion, with adjusted earnings up over 8% to $7.53 per share, this being quite a clean earnings number. Net debt of $1.50 billion was perfectly manageable, with EBITDA reported at $899 million for the year. The company guided for a modest 1-3% reported sales growth in 2025, driven by organic revenue growth, with adjusted earnings seen up modestly to $7.75 per share, plus or minus ten cents.

Following a strong first quarter, in which sales were up more than 5% the company maintained the guidance, with net debt flattish around $1.50 billion, as the company sees upside to the revenue guidance than to tariffs, but for now, expects a flattish and unchanged guidance with regard to its earnings.

Early in June, the company announced a more substantial deal, as the company announced an EUR 330 million deal to acquire German-based ELATEC GmbH. This firm is specialized in RFID credentials and reader solutions with an EUR 60-65 million sales contribution seen in 2026, for a 5.0-5.5 times forward sales multiple. The deal will be accretive to earnings that year, the extent of which is not specified, which is comforting as the business should be solidly profitable in that case, with the transaction already closing early in July.

With 87 million shares trading at $148, the market value comes in at $12.9 billion, for a $14.4 billion enterprise valuation. This results in a 3.8 times sales multiple awarded to Allegion, with the deal coming at a premium, adding about nearly 2% to total sales.

What Now?

The deal comes at quite an expense, as the deal comes in at nearly $400 million based on prevailing exchange rates, pushing up net debt towards $1.9 billion. With EBITDA reported at $900 million in 2024, and modest growth seen this year, leverage ratios are seen around 2 times.

Based on earnings power seen around $7.75 per share, after first quarter earnings were up thirty-one cents on the year before already, I see upside to the earnings guidance. Shares now trade at 19 times earnings, these being clean adjusted earnings, still resulting in a similar story as last December.

That said, I am growing more appreciative of the bolt-on M&A growth story of the business, certainly amidst modest leverage employed, and reasonable valuations after all. This makes me conclude to have continued interest in the shares and business, with a full intention to get involved on dips and covering future M&A events.
